data.title: Palantir: A $10 Billion Upside (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
My bull case on Palantir Technologies Inc. (NASDAQ:PLTR) was traditionally based on the commercial segment, particularly in the U.S. However, with the recent enterprise agreement with the U.S. Army, worth up to $10 billion for the next ten years, I am revisiting my thesis going into Q2 2025 earnings on August 4th.

While I still remain bullish on this stock (remember, Palantir redefined the Rule of 40, pushing it up to be the Rule of 80), I believe the government segment will be predominant in terms of revenue contribution and growth for the next few years. The downside of the commercial segment is the lack of growth outside of the U.S. This was particularly noticeable in the last quarter, when non-U.S. commercial revenue declined 5% YOY and 11% sequentially. On the other hand, non-U.S. government revenue was up by 45% YOY and 2% sequentially. Essentially, the non-U.S. government segment is growing at relatively the same pace as the U.S. government unit (45% YOY and 9% sequentially in the last quarter). Therefore, I expect the growth gap between government and commercial to persist.

I have now downgraded my rating to a Buy going into Q2 earnings, as I believe the massive deal with the U.S. Army will pressure (to a certain extent) margins. This is not great news for the FY 2025 operating income guidance ($1.711 billion — $1.723 billion), as a surprise to the downside could hurt the stock, similarly to what happened with Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) after its operating income guidance disappointed investors.

Why Commercial Is Behind Government

My bull case always leaned on the commercial segment, rather than the government one. That recently changed. Here's why.

First, I always argued that commercial has more long-term upside than the government from a growth perspective. The AIP platform can essentially be integrated by any corporation around the world, looking to right-size their headcount. I already discussed in a previous coverage why I believe agents will replace a significant number of jobs, particularly white collar ones.

So, the potential of the AIP platform is there. However, the adoption is quite anemic and doesn't quite live up to the hype, particularly outside of the U.S. Here is what CEO Alex Karp said in Q4 last year (emphasis added):

We see as much as I personally care about the broader West, including Continental Europe, despite our best efforts and working on every day, it's anemic. We're growing around 4% on 13% of our company.

He further emphasized in Q1 (emphasis added):

If you take the 90% of our business that is outside of Europe, which is going through very structural change and doesn't quite get AI, maybe in the near future will get AI, we are hoping and praying,

Karp doesn't have much hope in Europe (from a commercial perspective), and I confirm his disbelief, as I work for a large aerospace corporation on that continent that is not keen on AI. This drag is clearly noticeable in the Q1 earnings results. Non-U.S. commercial revenue declined 5% YOY and 11% sequentially, totaling $141 million.

As for U.S. commercial, there is indeed explosive growth (71% YOY in the past quarter), but the run rate of just $1 billion is still small compared to the upside that the AIP platform provides to enterprises. Furthermore, the backlog (Palantir calls it "TCV, total contract value") for U.S. commercial is just $810 million. Yes, that's 2.4x more than last year; however, the absolute value (i.e., $810 million) doesn't quite justify the premium at which the company is currently trading (P/E of 687).

Looking at the guidance for FY 2025, management expects the US commercial segment to grow by 68% YOY, with a midpoint revenue guidance of $ 1.178 billion. Again, great growth figures, but the absolute value ($1.18 billion) doesn't quite justify the valuation premium.

My next point refers to margins. Management is kind enough to share with us the contribution of each segment (in other words, the profitability) in their financial reporting.

As seen above, the margins of the commercial and the government segments are pretty much the same (61% and 62%, respectively). This is not what one would initially expect, as government contracts tend to last longer, and therefore, demand a volume-based discounted price. Back in February, I was quite certain that these discounts would further deepen after Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth ordered the Pentagon agencies and the military to cut down 8% of their spending, each year, for the next 5 years. That's $50 billion per year in savings.

So, I initially projected that margins would decline as the company reached a plateau in defense contracts. I was dead wrong.

Momentum In the Government Segment

Recently, the U.S. Army awarded Palantir a massive contract worth up to $10 billion over a period of 10 years. According to the press release (emphasis added):

The EA [Enterprise Agreement] will enhance military readiness and drive operational efficiency while delivering significant cost efficiencies.

Furthermore, the deal consolidates 75 contracts into a single one (emphasis added),

"accelerating the delivery of proven commercial software to warfighters while removing contract and re-seller pass-through fees."

Based on the words that I emphasized above, it appears that the army is looking to cut down costs with this contract, which is aligned with Hegseth's 8% cost-cutting goal that I mentioned in the previous section. To me, this massive $10 billion contract is the ultimate confirmation that Palantir's Foundry, Gotham, and Maven Smart System platforms are truly effective from a cost perspective. As you may already know, both Foundry and Gotham are using a single codebase, which means the Army doesn't pay for duplicate software licenses or parallel integration projects for each mission domain. Also, through Apollo’s license metering model, the Army only pays for active user seats and compute nodes.

This $10 billion deal comes on top of the Maven Smart System contract, worth $795 million, after an expansion was announced in May this year. I believe that in the next earnings call, we will get more information on whether the $10 billion contract consolidates the smaller Maven deal or whether they are separate from each other.

Other big contracts include the provision of the Maven Smart System to NATO for use across all 32 member states. This is great news for the non-U.S. government segment, which is growing at 45% YOY, unlike the recent decline in the non-U.S. commercial. So far, the growth seems to be driven by UK healthcare and defense contracts, along with the new Maven partnership with NATO.

In the U.S., aside from the massive $10 billion contract with the U.S. Army, the TITAN program has been a growth driver for Palantir, with the first TITAN vehicle delivered in Q1 2025. The current MSS (Maven Smart System) contract that I mentioned earlier runs through May 2029 and has a total contract ceiling of $1.275 billion ($480M + $795M).

Overall, I now see more upside in the government segment than in commercial, looking at a 1-2 year timeframe. Therefore, I expect the growth gap between Palantir's commercial and government segments to persist.

Risks

I believe the biggest risk to consider going into Q2 earnings comes from a margin perspective. There is something in the Army's press release that I didn't quite like:

The agreement establishes volume-based discounts for the contract’s performance period of up to 10 years.

Considering the timeframe of 10 years, those volume-based discounts could be hefty. While these contracts will support the revenue growth, currently in the mid-40s for the government segment, they will most likely pressure profitability.

So far, the margin for the government segment is quite impressive, sitting at 62% in Q1. However, the presence of low-margin revenue could depress that metric.

From a guidance perspective, the FY 2025 expectation for adj. operating income is between $1.711 billion — $1.723 billion. Below is the Street's expectation for this metric:

I see a risk of management being conservative from a profitability perspective due to the $10 contract with the Army. If their FY 2025 guidance for operating income disappoints the Street, we could be in for an unpleasant selloff, similar to what we have seen this week with Amazon.

Looking Ahead

Q2 earnings are right around the corner, with results coming out on August 4 after market close. I have no doubts that the main topic will be the $10 billion enterprise agreement contract with the U.S. Army. Therefore, I recommend keeping a close eye on any comments on how quickly they will be able to materialize the potential $10 billion into sales. At the end of Q1, Palantir's backlog was $5.97 billion, up 45% YOY and 10% sequentially. Even though the $10 billion contract announcement falls within Q3, analysts will most likely ask management to comment on how quickly the deal can flow into the income statement.

As for the guidance, I included below the expectations for Q2 and FY 2025.

I have a high conviction that the revenue guidance for FY 2025 will be upgraded, considering the recent deal with the Army. However, I cannot say the same for the US commercial segment.

As for the Street expectations for Q2 and Q3 this year, the EPS consensus is the same for both quarters, at 0.14.

In regard to revenue, the Street is expecting growth to be in the mid to high 30s.

Overall, I believe the company will beat these expectations, although I warn that the operating income guidance for FY 2025 could disappoint investors if the volume contracts within the $10 billion deal with the Army end up pressuring margins.

data.title: Vale: Undervalued Iron Giant Ready For The Next Cycle
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

Vale S.A. (NYSE:VALE) is one of the largest iron ore miners on the planet, with high-grade operations in Brazil but also Canada, Indonesia, Australia, among others. The company also has a focus on energy transition metals, being one of the top nickel producers globally, with significant exposure to copper as well, for which they have very significant investment plans in the future.

The stock has been following the price of iron well, as that remains their main source of income by far, and I believe the entire industry is approaching very interesting levels now, thanks to tariff uncertainty and the commodity reverting to its mean after a significant peak. Following a lot of volatility and now positive changes in China’s policy but also potential risk of tariffs and even a trade war, Vale’s stock is back to some of the lowest levels in the past half a decade, which is among the company’s lowest levels in the past 2 decades, only being toppled by commodity bottoms/recessions.

What they can make depends a lot on the price of iron, but they can deliver from $3-4 billion now on much higher CAPEX to even more than $12-16 billion in good years. For a roughly $42 billion market cap, that’s good in any scenario we look at, especially if we compare them to the competition, as you’ll see below.

They usually make pretty much the same free cash flow as Rio Tinto Group (RIO) - a $106 billion company - on a much lower revenue, meaning that the margin is significantly better. These numbers are now distorted by the high CAPEX, so we have to keep that in mind, while the 5Y averages are during some of the best years for the industry.

They see CAPEX of around $5.9 billion (vs. $6.5 billion estimated initially) this year compared to $5.67 billion in 2024, a good ~$2 billion of that going into copper, nickel and other energy transition metals. So, in the long term, this can be an even better way to get exposure to the global economy plus the energy transition.

In Q2, Vale says that their all-in costs were down 10% in iron ore (to US$ 55.3/t), a whopping 60% in copper (US$ 1,450/t), and a very solid 30% in nickel (US$ 12,396/t) year over year, as a result of the implementation of efficiency initiatives and higher production. This also allowed them to lower the guidance significantly on the copper all-in cost for 2025 to US$ 1,500-2,000/t (from US$ 2,800-3,300/t), thanks to solid performance and the jump in gold prices (a by-product; still goes into the calculation of the copper cost).

Financially, based on their latest report, we see a healthy balance sheet, with the current assets covering the current liabilities. There isn’t a lot of debt, although it went up by about $2 billion last year, since the company plans a lot of investments, plus other expenses that I’ll mention below. We see around $40.5 billion (up $6 billion YTD!) in equity on what was, a few months ago, when I bought, an almost identical market cap, currently being one of the best PB ratios (around 1 now) at least among the larger players. But, this and the low price to cash flow ratio and other metrics are normal for Brazil since the market sees higher political and economic risk, as I’ll mention in the Peer Comparison below.

Now, does Brazil come with a significantly higher risk than in China for example? Not necessarily, but right now Brazil has massive interest rates after some very significant hikes recently. What happens when they cut it and the currency potentially loses value against the USD - despite the exchange rate standing pretty much the same over the past half a decade? But, wouldn’t it also boost the economy at the same time and attract investments? What we know for sure is that this is uncertainty, which has an effect on the price. That also has an effect on Vale’s interest payments, by the way, although they get preferential debt from Brazil’s Development Bank (BNDES). But, anyway, a good balance sheet overall and not a lot of debt to worry about.

They also expanded their net debt target range to as much as $20 billion, to allow them to focus on the next cycle, while remaining committed to the dividend policy, not excluding the possibility of extraordinary dividends.

Dividends & Buybacks

The minimum dividend in Brazil is 25% of the profit (aka the Mandatory Dividend), but Vale usually pays a bit more if we look at their history. Something pretty typical for Brazilian commodity companies that you’ll see for Petróleo Brasileiro S.A. - Petrobras (PBR) as well, for example, is the very high dividend yield. When you are valued at 4-5x the cash flow or net income and have to pay at least a quarter of that, that by default implies massive yields. The only issue is the volatility in that profit, so that’s why we saw 10, 20, 40% yields before, but also 2%, 3%, 5%, etc. when they had issues. But, in my opinion, that volatility is actually the best thing about them. As we saw a decade ago, if we buy this during very depressed times, Vale can even pay 80% to even more than 100% yields in great years, plus the significant stock price appreciation and buybacks. Still, we shouldn’t account for this kind of dividend on average, since this example means buying at the bottom and enjoying some of their best years ever.

Still, a note about this, other than the years with no profit, a company can choose not to pay a dividend if it is justified by their financials/previous losses, according to Brazilian law - although that’s not the case for Vale.

Risks & Opportunities

Vale is exposed a lot to both internal and external politics. Internal poses its own challenges and uncertainty (depends on politics) - but we saw Trump take an aggressive stance against BRICS even before taking office. Last year, he threatened BRICS even with “100% tariffs” - and a few days ago, he threatened additional 10% tariffs on them. President Lula even vowed to fight the "tariff injustice" recently.

We already saw an increase in steel and aluminum tariffs twice, to 50%, and that’s not the only commodity he targeted. I get some tariffs on China and targeted ones to bring back some production and negotiate better, but commodities are different. You can’t just mine that commodity locally to offset the higher prices. Even if you have it, it can take ages and many billions of dollars to produce it, as the exploration-to-production time chart is often very extensive in the mining industry. Back in 2019-2022, Brazil accounted for about 53% of US iron ore imports, compared to 23% for Canada. That’s about 5.3% of the total iron ore consumed by the US coming from Brazil, so we are looking at Trump playing a very dangerous game.

On top of that, China remains basically the market maker in iron ore and steel, with ~70-80% of the total global imports. Any issues or real estate crashes, but also the recent incentives and good regulatory changes can make the demand go up or down. Even recently, we have the example of the Evergrande collapse fear that contributed to iron ore falling as much as 60% from its record high in May 2021 to September 2021.

A key feature of Vale is the very good commodity purity, and IOCJ for example comes only from Vale—the name literally stands for Iron Ore Carajás fines (which is Vale’s mine)—being one of the highest purities on the planet. It’s more expensive, but it also means lower purifying costs and a quicker process, but they also make BRBF or agglomerates, which are a mid-level option. Still, the company is now focusing more on a wider grade offering rather than a value-over-volume approach to adapt to the weaker steel mill margins and the fading willingness of clients to pay a premium for the quality, although they save on the processing cost.

There is also potential for Vale to expand even more in Australia if lower-purity and having China nearby become a necessity—there are plenty of options. Again, this shift won’t come overnight, so they have time AND money to adapt. Plus, South America has potential to be basically a hub for steel product manufacturing—even more than it currently is—and the region itself should also need a lot of it. They see a ~9.75% increase in iron ore production from 2024 to 2030, most of their long-term growth being fueled by copper and nickel expansion projects, and the potential price appreciation of their commodities, with copper production expected to grow by 30-40% by 2030.

A way to diversify is through copper and nickel, of which Vale is a major producer. We see a bunch of projects and plans to develop and these two commodities are key for EVs, batteries and electrification. Copper especially plays a crucial role in any development in any country and there is a ton of long-term potential especially from India and Africa. But, what all these commodities have in common, on top of the dependency on China is the direct correlation to the global economy. Right now, copper demand comes from Electrical/Electronics (~35-40%), Construction/Infrastructure (25-30%), Transportation (~15%) and others, which is also why copper is known as Doctor Copper, basically “diagnosing” the state of the global economy—although, to be fair, price up doesn’t necessarily mean the economy is doing well—that’s perhaps a bit outdated. I think the demand for it is a better indicator, and that usually also has an effect on the price, but there can be other factors as well.

So, Vale is a leader in 3 crucial commodities for our future, with extra potential from copper and nickel.

However, Vale reiterated recently that it’s interested in a spin-off of their nickel and copper business by 2027, if not sooner. The company has already made this split internally and sold 10% of the stake in the copper-nickel unit to Saudi Arabia for a whopping $2.5 billion. What this can result in is unlocking some value for shareholders, as the nickel-copper business could attract much better valuations than the slow and steady iron, as they are key to several long-term trends. If this stake is worth $25 billion in the market’s eyes (that’s if we extrapolate based on what Saudi Arabia paid), we’re talking about 60% in the market cap for something that’s accounting for less than 20% of the company’s revenue. Even half of that should be value for Vale, especially since we’re talking about assets in Canada, Brazil and Indonesia, not only Brazil.

This could help Vale invest the $30 billion they targeted to boost copper and nickel capacity. In the meantime, the company would focus on unlocking value for their Vale Base Metals by cutting costs and being open to considering an IPO, merger, or maintaining the status quo depending on market conditions.

Brazil also has something known as decharacterization, basically meaning revitalizing the environment when they finish the operations. Now, just imagine if this company didn’t have this kind of expenses affecting the margin, although it still has very good ones. This is part of the country-related risk, as Brazil, even if not a major shareholder, still owns Golden Shares that grant it veto rights on specific matters, on top of the legislative pressure it can exert on Vale.

As mentioned in the recent BHP Group Limited (BHP) stock analysis, another important aspect to keep an eye on is the Samarco dam failure (not their only dam failure, by the way), a joint venture between BHP and Vale in Brazil. While BHP and Vale reached a proposed $31.7 billion settlement with Brazilian authorities in October 2024, most of the Brazilian municipalities impacted actually rejected it in March 2025, meaning things may not be over despite the court clearing them of the charges (Federal Prosecutor's Office said it would appeal this ruling). Separately, a massive class action lawsuit against BHP in London concluded its liability trial in March 2025, and a new development in June 2025 means BHP faces a contempt of court hearing there for allegedly trying to prevent some lawsuits in the UK - with a very significant ~$47 billion class action lawsuit in the UK still active, where Vale and BHP agreed to share potential costs. Also, there's another case against Vale and Samarco's Dutch subsidiary in the Netherlands, with a hearing scheduled for this month. So, we’ve recently got some clarity in this regard, but it still remains an uncertainty, for now.

As I mentioned in the Rio Tinto Group (RIO) stock analysis published recently regarding lower grades - which are abundant in China and Australia and are generally cheaper but require higher refining costs - there’s been some news about China developing a technology that allows it to increase productivity “by 3,600 times” by shortening the refining time significantly while also allowing it to reach near-zero emissions.

Now, China has been boasting about this kind of development for ages at this point, and I’d first wait to see it become commercially viable on a larger scale before drawing any conclusions, as it remains a thing that still has to be proven. If anything, Australian iron ore would probably be less affected compared to the higher-grade that Brazil’s Vale S.A. (VALE) can mine. I think this has the potential to shift the dynamics a bit, potentially making South America into an even bigger hub of iron ore refining and processing, serving the North American and African markets in particular, instead of shipping the ore to China. Vale even owns the largest bulk carriers on the planet (Valemax ships) specifically to deliver iron ore to China, so you can imagine that this market is very important to it directly. Still, for Rio Tinto, that can lead to more demand for their lower-grade ore, so it’s not necessarily bad, although it could result in less demand overall since China has plenty of low-grade ore.

Peer Comparison

Comparing Vale to their closest peers, Rio Tinto Group (RIO), BHP Group Limited (BHP) and Fortescue Ltd (OTCQX:FSUMF)/(OTCQX:FSUGY), I reiterate that they are the cheapest from basically every valuation metric, with only Fortescue being near their levels in some aspects. However, this has always been the case for Vale. The others are mostly Australian, while Vale is Brazilian, which means a very different political/economic environment. My point is that just because they are cheaper than the competitors, it doesn’t necessarily make Vale cheap, as these prices are risk-adjusted, although I, personally, don’t consider Brazil to be that risky and I’ve had some very successful investments there over time.

Valuation

For Vale’s DCF, I’ll assume a starting free cash flow of $6 billion this year, as a result of the ~$225 million in higher CAPEX, the potential effect of tariffs, combined with lower iron ore prices this year. This FCF is projected to then grow at a 3% CAGR for the first 5 years, followed by a 2.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5, assuming relatively low growth in average prices and margins in everything. Assuming a 2% terminal growth rate and a WACC of 10% to be more conservative, and considering $14.6 billion in net debt along with 4.27 billion shares outstanding, the resulting DCF model yields an enterprise value of ~$78.62 billion. After subtracting net debt, this leads to an equity value of ~$64.02 billion, or an intrinsic value per share of about $14.99, which is over 50% above the current price.

Conclusions

Overall, I rate Vale a Buy now, being a healthy, resilient and profitable company that should continue to play a key role in the iron, nickel and copper industries. Upsides and downsides will come and go, but this company might be at least okay in almost any scenario given their low-cost, high-quality operations. The stock will definitely be very volatile, but I see that as an opportunity to get potentially one of the best investments, depending on the price. I know this volatility is not for everyone, so be very sure that you understand this kind of company very well before you even consider buying. It’s ultimately all about the risk and reward, which for me are very good if they continue to fall.

data.title: AI Revolution Should Make The Demand For Both Gold And Silver Surge
data.description:
Earlier in one of my articles about silver, I wrote about how industrial demand for the shiny grey metal should increase thanks to the AI boom. However, not only should the demand for silver rise as a result of artificial intelligence becoming part of our daily lives. Also gold, which is rarely used as an industrial metal, could benefit as well. In this analysis, I will explain how.

Recap of my previous article

In my previous article about silver and artificial intelligence, I wrote that silver prices could reach triple-digit valuations if the Fed eases its monetary policies.

Also, the green energy transition and AI technologies would raise the demand for the grey shiny metal.

Here is what I wrote about the industrial demand for silver, highly likely supported by the AI boom:

“…shipping growth for artificial intelligence servers and switches will increase by double digits over the next several years to stay in line with the evolution of AI algorithms. Demand is also expected to increase for silver-palladium Ag-Pd multi-layer ceramic capacitors (MLCCs) in high-power components.

Interestingly, artificial intelligence demand for precious metals will most significantly affect the silver market. That is because the shiny grey metal has the lowest electrical resistance among all metals at standard temperatures. Moreover, it is an essential component in many electronic applications. The metal is already in short supply due to rising demand in the green energy sector. So, silver demand will likely run hot over the next several decades.”

I admitted the key risks for silver, including higher interest rates, a stock market crash, and market speculations.

But let us have a look at the AI-related industrial demand for both gold and silver.

So, why is AI so good for the precious metals industrial demand?

Every AI data center, every high-performance GPU, and every cloud server stack depends on precious metals. When it comes to safety and conductivity, both gold and silver are irreplaceable.

As I have mentioned before, silver is used in semiconductors, 5G technologies, and EVs—these require 50 grams of silver per vehicle—MLCCs in high-power components, and photovoltaic cells that are present in data centers. So, silver industrial demand increased by 4% in 2024 to 680.5 million ounces, thus reaching a new record high for the fourth year in a row.

In other words, it is hard to meet this high demand. Indeed, silver is sometimes extracted for its own sake, but most people do not know that it is frequently mined as a byproduct of other metals such as copper, lead, and zinc. So, when the demand rises, the supply cannot immediately meet that demand increase. The extraction and mining volumes have decreased after COVID-19, and industry analysts forecast rising structural deficits by 2026.

Gold is vital to produce electronics that power AI. Such electronics include memory chips, sensors, and processors. These components are necessary to produce smartphones, self-driving cars, and even data centers. Thanks to its superior conductivity, gold stands here to gain because the shiny yellow metal makes sure that data can be processed and transmitted at high speed with little energy loss. Gold does not corrode, thus increasing electronic components’ service lives. Due to the exponential growth in the AI sector, the demand for components that use gold can only rise, thus further increasing the demand for the shiny yellow metal. Moreover, industries such as healthcare and finance actively use AI to increase efficiency and innovation, which can further increase demand.

Why is AI so good for investment demand for precious metals?

However, as I have mentioned many times before, gold’s main use is not industrial. Only about 10-12% of the gold mined each year is for industrial uses. A substantial amount of demand for gold, however, is due to investors. In other words, gold is money.

Among notable market analysts is Peter Schiff, who thinks that the AI boom requires plenty of expenditures, and a lion’s share of them would be due to the government because it is essential to subsidize businesses and also to build the appropriate infrastructure. As a result, money-printing by the Fed and other central banks all over the world would only rise to finance the AI revolution. Therefore, the value of fiat money should decrease. As I have mentioned many times before, the US national debt is growing fast. Between 2020 and 2022, the Fed added more than $4 trillion to its balance sheet. The AI revolution will likely provoke an even higher debt rise thanks to the heavy spending on the necessary infrastructure.

These ample amounts of money should be used somewhere. They will not only raise productivity and GDP as a result. They tend to pump asset bubbles. Due to the rising government debt and rising liquidity to finance it, inflation also increases.

Thanks to the fact that fiat money is losing its value, gold seems to be the only real asset because it does not depend on counteragents. So, it is associated with stability and is also a hedge against fiat currencies’ devaluation.

In 2024, central banks bought over 1,000 metric tons of gold—for the third consecutive year, that is. This is remarkable because for decades, central banks were net sellers of gold. Silver is also highly likely to benefit as a result because not all private investors can afford to buy large quantities of gold. This is especially true of developing markets. Moreover, thanks to its great volatility, silver would be even more likely to appreciate even more than gold.

Interestingly, AI systems that create industrial demand for both gold and silver also help market participants to trade gold and silver. AI platforms scan social media and analyze central banks’ protocols, and it takes them seconds to buy and sell various assets. This leads to volatility. Silver is particularly sensitive here because the market for silver is much smaller. So, any bullish twit or geopolitical news can lead to a substantial price surge or crash, which in turn might give long-term investors an excellent opportunity to start a position in silver.

Gold, however, is less sensitive to such wild price swings caused by AI thanks to central banks’ interventions and higher liquidity. But bear in mind that during market panic even gold can substantially fluctuate in value thanks to AI trading. So, investors should prepare for greater volatility ahead.

Gold and silver valuations

The gold market capitalization-to-US stock market capitalization ratio is now near the median level but substantially below the highs reached in the 1930s-1940s, 1980, and 2011. In fact, the diagram below suggests that gold is cheap.

Also interesting is the overall commodities-to-stocks relationship—the S&P GSCI Total Return Index to the S&P 500. The GSCI Commodity Index (TR) has barely recovered from its historical low in April 2020. The ratio is now substantially below the long-term median of 3.93.

The gold-to-silver ratio is also reasonably high, suggesting silver's slight undervaluation relative to gold. Right now the ratio is lingering near 90.

Downside risks

Among the downside risks to my thesis is investors’ enthusiasm with other asset classes apart from precious metals. As it was during the dot-com bubble, speculators all rushed to buy high-tech stocks while ignoring high-value assets, including gold. This is a particularly likely scenario if the Fed manages to avoid an economic crisis in the near future. Right now, the Fed is likely to ease its monetary policies by the end of this year, which is favorable for both high-risk assets and precious metals.

Also, geopolitical stability is a significant risk factor for precious metals and gold in particular. However, this is not my base-case scenario because there are significant geopolitical risks, including trade tensions and the war conflicts in the Middle East and also between Russia and Ukraine.

Conclusion

Artificial intelligence will be a great stimulus for both gold and silver markets. There are industrial uses of both gold and silver in AI, but the most significant, in my view, will be the indirect effect of AI on both gold and silver. The AI revolution will require a lot of money printing by central banks, and therefore there will be higher demand for precious metals to hedge investors’ savings from inflation. Moreover, gold and silver still seem to be good value for money relative to equities. The risks to my bullish case are the strong global economy and lack of geopolitical conflicts.

data.title: Labrador Iron: Other Than That, How Was The Play?
data.description:
Note: All amounts are in Canadian Dollars unless noted otherwise. Canadian stock options are inaccessible by US Investors.

We have covered Labrador Iron Ore Royalty Corp. (TSX:LIF:CA), a few times over the years. We have recommended that while the company was attractive, the macro was not. This negative view has worked out with Labrador underperforming even cash over the past 30 months.

We give investors another view into our thinking today.

Update

We love royalty plays and have held many of them over the years. We are actually buying two in very large amounts just in the last week. So please do not construe our work on Labrador as reflective of a bias of sorts. We have not purchased the stock, as every single time we looked at it, we saw problems at a macro level. Iron ore is primarily used in making steel.

About half of the world's steel is consumed by China.

This number used to actually be much higher when we first started looking at Labrador but has moved down as China's woes have continued. The bulk of China's steel is used in residential construction though the more recent data has been very blurry. As we have previously mentioned, our reason for staying out of this has been an imploding housing market in China. Let's see how that is shaping up.

BEIJING, July 15 (Reuters) - China's new home prices fell at the fastest monthly pace in eight months in June, highlighting the struggle to revive demand despite repeated policy measures and growing calls for additional support.

The 0.3% month-on-month drop, calculated by Reuters based on data released by the National Bureau of Statistics, extended a weak trend that has persisted since May 2023. Prices fell 0.2% on month in May.

On an annual basis, new home prices in June declined 3.2%, versus a 3.5% drop in May.

The property sector, which accounted for about a quarter of economic activity prior to its meltdown roughly four years ago, remains a heavy drag on economic growth. That has complicated policymakers' efforts to achieve a target of "around 5%" GDP growth amid factory-gate deflation, subdued consumer demand and geopolitical tensions with the United States.

Source: Reuters (emphasis ours)

As usual, that led to calls for (you guessed it), more stimulus.

The peak season for home purchases in China is around March and April.

The post-peak slowdown has revealed fresh market vulnerabilities, stemming from policy impacts, demand fluctuations, regional disparities and weakened confidence, said Zhang Dawei, chief analyst at Centaline Property Agency, who stressed the need for additional supportive measures.

Source: Reuters

When there is enough extra housing in China to house the entire population of US, we are unsure how stimulus will improve demand for housing. As expected property sector investment is now moving lower sharply.

BEIJING, July 15 (Reuters) - Property investment in China declined 11.2% in the first half of the year from the same period last year, after slumping 10.7% in January-May, official data showed on Tuesday.

Property sales by floor area fell 3.5% year-on-year, compared with a 2.9% drop in the first five months of the year.

New construction starts measured by floor area dropped 20%, after contracting 22.8% in the January-to-May period.

Source: Reuters (emphasis ours)

What is actually shocking to us is that even a single home is now being constructed. We are not exaggerating. Combine the data above with the data below and tell me you would want to build a single home in China.

China’s real-estate bust left behind tens of millions of empty housing units. Now that historic glut of unoccupied property is colliding with China’s shrinking population , leaving cities stuck with homes they might never be able to fill.

The country could have as many as 90 million empty housing units, according to a tally of economists’ estimates. Assuming three people per household, that’s enough for the entire population of Brazil.

Filling those homes would be hard enough even if China’s population were growing, but it’s not. Because of the country’s one-child policy , it is expected to fall by 204 million people over the next 30 years.

Source: Kanebridge News (emphasis ours)

It actually gets worse. Those 90 million units don't count units under construction.

Beginning in 2015, a widening gap emerges—now totaling 10,345 million square meters of started but uncompleted housing as of May 2025. At an avg apartment size of 81.5 sqm, that translates to 126 million unfinished units—roughly equivalent to the entire housing stock of the United States.”

Source: Macro Business Australia (emphasis ours)

Goldman Sachs (GS) joined us in donning the Captain Obvious hat.

China's property crisis is in its fourth year, and the market is still far from a bottom, Goldman Sachs analysts wrote in a report on Wednesday.

Home prices have fallen 20% over four years and could decline another 10% before bottoming out in 2027, they wrote.

Goldman Sachs' report was based on an analysis of housing bust episodes across 15 economies since 1960, which found that the median housing price correction is 30% over six years. Goldman Sachs defines housing busts as a decline of 20% from cyclical peaks.

"Given the durability of housing stock and stickiness of house prices, it may take years for housing busts to finally find a bottom," the analysts wrote.

Source: Business Insider

So for us, this is all there is. There is no dividend analysis, NAV measurement, management competence to look at for Labrador. That is fairly rare as in almost all cases, macro plays some role, but never dominates like this. Ignoring the macro would be like asking the question in the title. Steel consumption likely drops every single year until China becomes a far smaller piece of the pie chart we showed earlier.

Verdict

Labrador is now cheaper than it has been for most of the time that we covered it. Normally, around here, we would have started to buy a position, with the help of defensive (in-the-money, longer dated) covered calls. But the stock does not look remotely cheap considering the macro headwinds. The stock does not look cheap on a price to sales multiple (note how low it got in 2016 and 2020) either.

Iron ore is also a commodity with very low costs to produce. By our estimate, the price would have to drop 40% from here, for global production to be cut by 20%. The bulk of the mines are profitable on a cash cost basis well below even $80/ton.

We remain skeptical that you can make money here. Stay out.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
